text: The separation of propylene from propane using high efficiency adsorption is a highly desired alternative to the existing and energy intensive cryogenic distillation technology. High selectivity using the molecular sieving properties of adsorbents is a target but the low capacity that generally limits the effectiveness of such adsorbents remains an unsolved challenge. Here, we synthesized a novel oxyfluoride metal-organic framework UTSA-400: Ni(WO2F4)(pyz)2 with ultramicropores consisting of one-dimensional channels in which propylene can fit efficiently while at the same time excluding propane molecules in a preferential interaction, hence achieving superior affinity. Further, pairs of oxide/fluoride on the surface of the structure further enhance propylene affinity thus leading to a much higher uptake than observed in other microporous adsorbents toward achieving full adsorption close to saturation of propylene in the micropores and, hence, near the full pore utilization as well as high column packing densities. In situ characterization by in situ IR coupled with dispersion-corrected density functional theory (DFT) calculations at the atomic level explain the effects that cause enhancements in UTSA-400 toward adsorption. Column breakthrough experiments result in the direct production of polymer grade (>99.5%) propylene at dynamic productivity. UTSA-400 is another example of breakthrough adsorbents with pore engineering at atomic scale to realize new separations where the pre-requisites that restrict separation effectiveness at the adsorption level can be resolved to provide highly efficient adsorptive separation for industrially relevant and commercially viable application of great economic values and benefit.
explanation: Extremely technical and cohesive with domain-specific detail; it reads like a polished abstract with marketing-style phrasing. The absence of citations and highly generalized claims slightly increase the likelihood of AI generation, though a real publication could resemble this as well.
